distractors.C: Higher unemployment is more often associated with rising crime, not reductions.
distractors.D: Retrenchment typically worsens income inequality, not improves it.

questions.stem: Money is long-lasting, easy to carry around and can be changed into smaller denominations. Which of the following characteristics of money are being described in the statement above?
questions.explanation: Long-lasting describes durability, easy to carry describes portability, and ability to be changed into smaller denominations describes divisibility.

distractors.A: Liquidity is not specifically described by the statement.
distractors.B: Scarcity and uniformity are not the features mentioned in the statement.
distractors.D: Uniformity is not described in the statement.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the financial sector receiving savings/deposits from firms/households and channelling them as loanable funds for investment/spending back to firms/households. The diagram BEST describes the

options.B: flow of deposits into the financial sector
options.C: flow of funds into the household sector
options.D: role that the financial sector performs for customers

questions.explanation: The diagram illustrates financial intermediation: the financial sector takes savings/deposits and channels them as loanable funds for investment and spending, which describes the role financial institutions perform for their customers.

distractors.A: The diagram is about the financial sector overall, not the specific functions of the central bank.
distractors.B: It shows inflows AND outflows, not only deposits flowing in.
distractors.C: The funds flow back to firms and households, not only into the household sector.

questions.stem: A group of workers who agree to negotiate with employers as one collective unit rather than individually is referred to as a
questions.explanation: A trade union is an organisation of workers that negotiates collectively with employers on issues such as wages and working conditions.

distractors.B: A lobby group seeks to influence policy, not negotiate employment terms.
distractors.C: A credit union provides savings and loan services to its members.
distractors.D: A financial group is a corporate or investment grouping, not a workers' negotiating body.

questions.stem:
The table below shows manufacturing output as a share of gross domestic product (GDP) for four countries, A, B, C and D.

YearCountry
ABCD
201628%40%29%29%
201723%34%26%28%
201819%31%21%23%

The table shows that ALL countries experienced

options.A: a decline in the gross domestic product
options.B: a decline in the sale of manufactured products
options.C: an increase in employment in the manufacturing sector
options.D: a decline in manufacturing output as a share of gross domestic product

questions.explanation: For every country, the percentage share of manufacturing in GDP falls each year from 2016 to 2018, indicating a decline in manufacturing's share of GDP.

distractors.A: The table only shows the share of GDP, not whether GDP itself fell.
distractors.B: The table does not directly measure sales of manufactured products.
distractors.C: A falling share of GDP typically suggests less, not more, manufacturing employment.

questions.stem: Which of the following factors cause(s) money to lose value and become less effective as a medium of exchange?
questions.explanation: Inflation reduces the purchasing power of money over time. As prices rise, each unit of money buys less, undermining money's role as a medium of exchange.

distractors.B: Market failure refers to inefficient resource allocation, not loss of money's value.
distractors.C: Unemployment is a labour-market problem and does not by itself erode money's value.
distractors.D: Negative externalities are spillover costs of transactions, not a cause of currency devaluation.

questions.stem: A diagram illustrates the production possibility frontier for an economy in Year 1 (AA) and Year 2 (BB), where BB lies inside AA. From the diagram, it can be deduced that

options.A: Point X was unattainable in Year 2
options.B: Point X was efficient in Year 1 and Year 2
options.C: there was decreasing opportunity cost in Year 2
options.D: there was economic growth between Year 1 and Year 2

questions.explanation: When the PPF shifts inward from AA to BB, the economy's productive capacity has decreased. A point such as X that lies on the outer (Year 1) frontier becomes unattainable on the inner (Year 2) frontier.

distractors.B: Point X is on the Year 1 frontier but lies outside the Year 2 frontier, so it cannot be efficient in Year 2.
distractors.C: An inward shift does not imply decreasing opportunity cost.
distractors.D: An inward shift represents economic decline, not growth.

questions.stem: A transfer as recorded in the balance of payments is

options.A: a maternity benefit received at home by the local government agency
options.B: an unemployment benefit received by citizens in their country by their government
options.C: a subsidy for a local firm to encourage increased production for the export market
options.D: a donation by a foreign government of relief supplies to a country following the passage of a hurricane

questions.explanation: Transfers in the balance of payments are unrequited payments between countries, such as foreign aid or relief supplies sent from one country to another. Only option D involves a cross-border transfer.

distractors.A: A domestic maternity benefit is an internal transaction, not part of the balance of payments.
distractors.B: Unemployment benefits paid by a government to its own citizens are domestic, not international, transfers.
distractors.C: A government subsidy to a local firm is a domestic policy measure, not a balance-of-payments transfer.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the circular flow of income between households and firms. Line X, which is labelled 'Payment for goods and services' and flows from households to firms, represents payment
questions.explanation: In the basic circular flow, households pay firms for goods and services purchased in the product market. This payment flow on the line labelled 'payment for goods and services' represents consumer expenditure on goods.

distractors.B: Payments to factors of production flow from firms to households, not the other way.
distractors.C: Tax payments go to the government, not to firms in the basic two-sector flow.
distractors.D: Loan repayments go to financial institutions, not to firms in this flow.

questions.stem: Between 2008 and 2018, Country X imported 500 000 cars from the United States. This represented an increase of 100 per cent over the previous ten-year period. As a result, the demand for US dollars increased significantly while exports to the US increased by 2 per cent. The scenario described above is MOST likely to result in

options.A: the value of the US dollar falling
options.B: the value of the US dollar remaining constant
options.C: Country X's dollar appreciating against the US dollar
options.D: Country X's dollar depreciating against the US dollar

questions.explanation: Increased demand for US dollars (to buy imports) with little extra supply of US dollars (from exports) causes the US dollar to appreciate and Country X's dollar to depreciate against it.

distractors.A: Higher demand for the US dollar causes it to rise, not fall.
distractors.B: With significantly higher demand, the US dollar value would not remain constant.
distractors.C: Country X's currency would weaken, not strengthen, against the US dollar.

questions.stem: Country A is experiencing a balance of payments deficit. Which of the following measures can it take to reduce the deficit in the short-term?

options.C: Introduce policies to reduce the level of imports
options.D: Engage in trade with other low-cost producers of goods

questions.explanation: A balance of payments deficit can be reduced in the short term by introducing policies (such as tariffs or import restrictions) that reduce the level of imports.

distractors.A: Reducing exports would worsen the deficit by lowering foreign-currency earnings.
distractors.B: Higher imports would deepen the deficit.
distractors.D: Trading with low-cost producers may encourage more imports, worsening the deficit.

questions.stem: Which of the following products is NOT considered to be primary?
questions.explanation: Primary products are raw materials taken directly from nature. Bauxite, banana and petroleum fit this description. Furniture is a manufactured (secondary) product made from primary materials such as timber.

distractors.A: Bauxite is an extracted mineral, a primary product.
distractors.B: Bananas are an agricultural primary product.
distractors.D: Petroleum is extracted from the earth and is a primary product.

questions.stem: The term 'e-commerce' refers to
questions.explanation: 'E-commerce' is short for electronic commerce, the buying and selling of goods and services using electronic networks, especially the internet.

distractors.A: Ecotourism is environmentally responsible tourism, not e-commerce.
distractors.B: Export businesses sell goods abroad and need not be electronic.
distractors.C: Economies of scale refer to cost advantages from large-scale production.

questions.stem: Dennis grows apples and bananas but he is not using the available tools to get the maximum number of apples and bananas. This indicates that Dennis is
questions.explanation: Productive efficiency occurs when resources are used to produce the maximum possible output. Because Dennis is not getting the maximum output from his available tools, he is producing inside the production possibility frontier and is therefore inefficient.

distractors.A: Saving relates to setting aside income, not to the use of productive tools.
distractors.B: Efficiency would mean he is getting the MAXIMUM output, which is the opposite of the case described.
distractors.D: Being an economist is a profession; it does not describe the productive outcome shown.

questions.stem: A diagram shows a curve drawn between Good X (on the vertical axis) and Good Y (on the horizontal axis) for a certain country, with the country using all of its resources along the curve. What name is given to the curve represented above?
questions.explanation: A curve showing the maximum combinations of two goods that a country can produce when all of its resources are fully and efficiently employed is the production possibility curve (PPC) or production possibility frontier.

distractors.A: A demand curve plots price against quantity demanded, not two goods.
distractors.B: A market supply curve plots price against total quantity supplied of one good.
distractors.C: An individual supply curve also plots price against quantity supplied for one producer.

questions.stem: In Country N, the government provides essential goods such as water, electricity and housing for its citizens. However, other goods are provided by private companies. To which economic system does Country N MOST likely belong?
questions.explanation: A mixed economy combines features of the free-market and the planned economy: the government supplies essential goods/services (often merit goods and public utilities) while private firms supply most other goods. Country N matches this description.

distractors.A: A pure free market has no government provision of goods.
distractors.B: Traditional economies are based on custom and barter, not on a mix of government and private provision.
distractors.C: A planned economy has the government providing essentially ALL goods, not only some.

questions.stem: John, Mary and Don eat bread. John's income does not change so he buys the same amount of bread. Mary's income increases so she buys more bread. Don's income decreases so he buys less bread. The demand curve for bread shifts for
questions.explanation: A demand curve shifts when a non-price determinant such as income changes. John's income is unchanged so his demand curve does not shift. Mary's and Don's incomes have changed (so their demand curves shift right and left respectively). Hence the shift applies to Mary and Don.

distractors.A: John's income did not change, so his demand curve does not shift.
distractors.C: John's curve does not shift; only Mary's does in this pair.
distractors.D: John is incorrectly included; his demand curve does not shift.

questions.stem: Hurricanes cause irreparable damage to equipment and buildings, and sometimes result in loss of life. Which of the following factors of production are mentioned in the statement above?
questions.explanation: Buildings and equipment are man-made resources used in production, which classifies them as capital. Loss of life refers to people, the human effort or labour. Therefore the factors mentioned are labour and capital.

distractors.A: Land is not mentioned (no reference to natural resources like soil or minerals).
distractors.B: Loss of life is labour, not land.
distractors.D: Equipment and buildings are capital, not entrepreneurship.

questions.stem: The tools and machinery used by a country to produce goods in an economy is classified as
questions.explanation: Capital refers to man-made resources used in the production of goods and services. Tools and machinery are textbook examples of capital goods.

distractors.A: Land refers to natural resources, not man-made tools.
distractors.C: Labour refers to human effort, not physical equipment.
distractors.D: Entrepreneurial talent is the ability to combine the other factors and take risks, not the tools themselves.

questions.stem: Jane pays $2 to run in the park. The price for running increases to $4 so Jane now walks. Which of the following factors shift(s) Jane's demand for walking?
questions.explanation: Running and walking are substitutes. When the price of running (a related good) increases, Jane switches to walking. Her demand curve for walking shifts to the right because of a change in the price of a related/substitute good.

distractors.A: Jane's income is not mentioned as having changed.
distractors.B: There is no mention of future expectations.
distractors.C: Her tastes have not changed; she is responding to a price change in a substitute.

questions.stem:
The following table shows the price and quantity of a product.

| Price | $35 | $50 | $60 |
| Quantity | 9 | 7 | 3 |

Which of the following curves can be plotted using the information above?
questions.explanation: The table shows that as the price of the product rises ($35 to $60), the quantity falls (9 to 3). This inverse relationship between price and quantity is the law of demand, so the table can be used to plot a demand curve.

distractors.A: Total cost relates output to cost, not price to quantity demanded.
distractors.B: Fixed cost does not vary with output and cannot be plotted from this data.
distractors.C: Variable cost relates cost to output level, not price to quantity.

questions.stem: Travis and his friend sell candies to their classmates during lunch time at school. This scenario is an example of a
questions.explanation: A market is any arrangement that brings buyers and sellers together to exchange goods or services. Travis and his friend (sellers) interact with classmates (buyers) to exchange candies for money - this is a market.

distractors.A: A union is an organisation of workers, unrelated to this trade.
distractors.C: A supply schedule is a table showing price-quantity supplied relationships.
distractors.D: A demand schedule is a table showing price-quantity demanded relationships.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the demand and supply of grapes, with prices p1, p2, p3, p4 marked on the vertical (price) axis and quantities q1, q2, q3, q4 marked on the horizontal (quantity) axis. Demand and supply intersect at the equilibrium. What is the equilibrium price and quantity demanded?
questions.explanation: The equilibrium price and quantity are read at the intersection of the demand and supply curves. In the diagram, demand and supply cross at the middle reference lines, giving equilibrium price p2 and equilibrium quantity q2.

distractors.A: p4 and q1 represent a price above equilibrium and a low quantity demanded - this is a surplus, not equilibrium.
distractors.C: p3 and q3 are not the coordinates where D and S intersect in the diagram.
distractors.D: p1 and q4 represent a low price and high quantity demanded - this is a shortage, not equilibrium.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the price of an item and the quantity demanded and supplied before and after the item is subsidized. S is the supply curve before a subsidy is given. As a result of the granting of the subsidy, it is expected that the
questions.explanation: A subsidy lowers producers' costs of production, encouraging them to supply more at every price. This shifts the supply curve to the right (S to Ss in the diagram), so supply increases and the price typically falls.

distractors.A: A subsidy lowers, not raises, the equilibrium price.
distractors.C: Supply rises (rightward shift) under a subsidy; it does not decrease.
distractors.D: A subsidy affects supply, not demand; the demand curve does not shift.

questions.stem: A diagram represents the market demand for bananas, with curves K and J intersecting on a price-quantity graph. The intersection of K and J is called
questions.explanation: The point where the demand curve and supply curve intersect on a market diagram is called the market equilibrium - the price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.

distractors.A: A surplus exists ABOVE the equilibrium price, not at the intersection.
distractors.B: A shortage exists BELOW the equilibrium price, not at the intersection.
distractors.D: Disequilibrium is any point off the intersection, not on it.

questions.stem: A tropical disease that destroys banana production is likely to

options.A: reduce the equilibrium price and reduce the quantity
options.B: increase the equilibrium price and increase the quantity
options.C: reduce the equilibrium price and increase the quantity
options.D: increase the equilibrium price and reduce the quantity

questions.explanation: A disease that destroys banana production reduces supply (supply curve shifts left). With demand unchanged, the new equilibrium has a higher price and a lower quantity traded.

distractors.A: A leftward supply shift raises price, not lowers it.
distractors.B: Quantity falls (less is supplied), not rises.
distractors.C: Both signs are wrong - price rises and quantity falls.

questions.stem: Nitya's income increases from $100 to $150. The quantity of shirts she purchased increased by 100%. Her income elasticity of demand for shirts is
questions.explanation: % change in income = (150 - 100)/100 x 100% = 50%. % change in quantity demanded = 100%. YED = 100% / 50% = 2.00.

distractors.A: 1.50 would come from a 75% rise in quantity demanded, not 100%.
distractors.B: 1.75 does not correspond to any correct calculation here.
distractors.D: 2.75 does not match the YED formula with these numbers.

questions.stem: Imran sells flour and baking powder. Renee is a baker who buys flour from Imran. The price of salt increases so Renee buys less flour. Which of the following curves shifts?

options.D: Imran's supply curve for baking powder

questions.explanation: Salt and flour are complements in baking. When the price of salt rises, less salt is bought, and the demand for the complementary good (flour) falls at every price. Renee's demand curve for flour therefore shifts to the left.

distractors.A: Imran's costs/conditions of supply have not changed; his supply curve does not shift.
distractors.B: A rise in the price of salt causes a movement along the salt demand curve, not a shift of it.
distractors.D: Baking powder's market is not directly affected in the scenario.

questions.stem: The market for school bags is perfectly competitive. If one seller leaves the market, the price of school bags will
questions.explanation: In perfect competition there are very many small sellers, each supplying a negligible fraction of the market. The exit of one seller has no measurable impact on market supply, so the market price does not change.

distractors.A: One seller leaving is too small a change to push up market price.
distractors.B: An exit reduces (not increases) supply, but in any case the impact is negligible.
distractors.D: Perfect competition produces a stable equilibrium price, not an unstable one.

questions.stem: PowerUp is the only supplier of electricity in Westville. It charges the highest price for electricity compared to other electric companies in the Caribbean. This scenario describes an example of market failure caused by
questions.explanation: PowerUp is the ONLY supplier of electricity in Westville, which is the defining feature of a monopoly. The monopoly position enables PowerUp to charge an excessively high price - a classic source of market failure due to lack of competition.

distractors.A: A negative externality is a spillover cost on third parties; nothing in the scenario describes this.
distractors.B: 'Greedy supplier' is not a recognised economic source of market failure.
distractors.C: Electricity is a private good provided by a firm, not a public good (which is non-excludable and non-rival).

questions.stem: The provision of public goods creates
questions.explanation: Public goods (e.g. street lighting, national defence) confer benefits on people other than those who pay directly for them. These spill-over benefits to third parties are positive externalities.

distractors.A: Public goods do generate spill-over effects, so externalities are not absent.
distractors.C: Negative externalities involve third-party harms (e.g. pollution), not benefits.
distractors.D: 'Government externalities' is not a standard economic term.

questions.stem: Which of the following will occur when the central bank increases the reserve requirement?

options.A: Bank loans decrease and the money supply decreases.
options.B: Bank loans decrease and the money supply increases.
options.C: Bank loans increase and the money supply increases.
options.D: Bank loans increase and the money supply decreases.

questions.explanation: Raising the reserve requirement forces commercial banks to hold a larger share of deposits as reserves, leaving less available to lend. Loans therefore decrease, and because loans create deposits, the money supply also decreases.

distractors.B: Less lending reduces, not increases, the money supply.
distractors.C: Banks cannot lend more when forced to hold more reserves.
distractors.D: Loans fall (not rise) and the money supply falls with them.

questions.stem: A diagram shows funds flowing from SAVERS to X, and from X to BORROWERS, with each arrow labelled 'Funds'. What economic agent is represented by X?
questions.explanation: The diagram shows the financial intermediation role: an entity that accepts funds from savers and channels them to borrowers. This is the defining function of financial institutions (e.g. commercial banks, credit unions).

distractors.A: Households are typically savers/borrowers in the diagram, not the intermediary.
distractors.B: The government borrows but does not act as the channel between general savers and borrowers.
distractors.C: The central bank deals with commercial banks, not directly between general savers and borrowers.

questions.stem: An institution that provides protection against sudden losses is called
questions.explanation: An insurance company pools premiums from many policyholders and pays out to those who suffer specified losses, thus providing protection against sudden, unforeseen losses.

distractors.A: Building societies primarily provide mortgage loans for housing.
distractors.B: Commercial banks accept deposits and grant loans but do not pay out for losses.
distractors.D: Investment trust companies pool funds for investment, not for loss protection.

questions.stem: Aaron gets two $1 notes from the central bank. He keeps one and gives the other to Nancy. Nancy complains that the $1 notes are not the same. Nancy does not understand that the two $1 notes are
questions.explanation: For money to function effectively, every note of the same denomination must be identical in value - that is, homogeneous (uniform/uniformity). Although the two $1 notes may differ slightly in appearance (e.g. serial numbers, wear), they have the same value because money is homogeneous.

distractors.A: Durability means money lasts a long time - this is not what Nancy fails to understand.
distractors.B: Portability means money can be easily carried.
distractors.C: Acceptability refers to being widely accepted as payment.

questions.stem: Carla sells Pablo a car and tells him that he can pay for it when he starts to work. Which function of money is being described?
questions.explanation: When payment for a current transaction is delayed to a future date, money is functioning as a standard of deferred payment - it allows debts and future payments to be recorded and settled later.

distractors.A: Store of value refers to holding wealth over time without losing purchasing power.
distractors.B: Measure of value (unit of account) is using money to price goods, not to delay payment.
distractors.C: Medium of exchange refers to immediate use of money in transactions, not deferred payments.

questions.stem:
The following information is given:

| Consumer consumption | $800 |
| Government spending | $300 |
| Gross investment | $100 |
| Exports of goods | $80 |
| Imports of goods | $50 |
| Income coming into the country | $80 |
| Income going out of the country | $70 |

The gross domestic product (GDP) is
questions.explanation: GDP using the expenditure approach is C + I + G + (X - M) = 800 + 100 + 300 + (80 - 50) = 800 + 100 + 300 + 30 = $1230. Income flows in and out of the country are used for GNP, not GDP.

distractors.A: $800 is only consumer consumption.
distractors.B: $940 omits some expenditure components.
distractors.C: $1200 uses X but not (X - M), or excludes some component.

questions.stem: A simple circular flow of income diagram between Firms and Households shows a labelled flow Y running from Firms to Households. The nominal flow labelled Y refers to
questions.explanation: In the simple two-sector circular flow, the flow from firms to households (the nominal/money flow) is the payment for the factors of production - i.e. factor incomes (wages, rent, interest, profit). Households supply factors of production to firms; firms pay factor incomes back to households.

distractors.A: Savings are a leakage out of the circular flow, not the firms-to-households flow itself.
distractors.B: Investment is spending by firms on capital, an injection, not the firms-to-households payment.
distractors.D: Factors of production are the REAL flow (resources) from households to firms; Y is the corresponding MONEY flow.

questions.stem: In Jared's first year of high school, he buys a pair of shoes for $40. In his last year, he buys a similar pair of shoes but pays $20 more. Which of the following economic situations did Jared experience?
questions.explanation: The general price of a similar good has risen from $40 to $60 (an increase of $20 = 50%) over time. A sustained increase in the general price level is inflation.

distractors.A: Growth refers to rising real GDP, not rising prices of a single good.
distractors.B: Saving is setting income aside, not paying more for goods.
distractors.D: Deflation is a fall in the general price level - the opposite of what Jared experienced.

questions.stem: After quitting his job, Ari found a job online two days later. Which type of unemployment did Ari experience?
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment is the short period of unemployment a worker experiences while moving between jobs. Ari was unemployed for only two days during a job search - the textbook example of frictional unemployment.

distractors.A: Cyclical unemployment is caused by economic downturns, not personal job-search gaps.
distractors.C: Structural unemployment lasts a long time and is caused by skills mismatch.
distractors.D: Real-wage (classical) unemployment is caused by wages being above the equilibrium level.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be considered a cause of cyclical unemployment?
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment arises during downturns in the business cycle (recessions), when aggregate demand and economic activity fall. Fluctuations in overall economic activity are therefore the cause of cyclical unemployment.

distractors.B: Deindustrialization causes structural unemployment.
distractors.C: Job searching causes frictional unemployment.
distractors.D: High wages above equilibrium cause real-wage (classical) unemployment.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a capital account transaction?

options.A: Jane sells flowers to tourists in Summerville.
options.B: Tourists visit Summerville to enjoy sandy beaches.
options.C: Jack sends money to his mother in another country.
options.D: An overseas company pays the building costs for local hotels.

questions.explanation: The capital account records cross-border transactions involving real and financial assets (such as foreign direct investment in buildings or capital goods). An overseas company financing the construction of local hotels is a foreign direct investment - a capital account inflow.

distractors.A: Selling flowers (a good) to tourists is recorded in the current account (exports of goods).
distractors.B: Tourist expenditure on services is a current account (services) transaction.
distractors.C: Personal remittances are usually recorded as current transfers in the current account.

questions.stem: Free trade is based on the principle of
questions.explanation: The economic argument for free trade is built on the principle of comparative advantage (Ricardo): countries should specialise in producing goods in which they have the lowest opportunity cost and trade for the rest, so that total world output and welfare increase.

distractors.A: 'Export advantage' is not a recognised theory of trade.
distractors.B: Production possibility is a concept about output combinations, not the basis for trade.
distractors.C: 'Substantive advantage' is not an established trade principle.

questions.stem: Jessica lives in Caribville and sells pencils to John who lives abroad. The sale of pencils will

options.A: increase Caribville's exports and increase its trade balance
options.B: increase Caribville's exports and decrease its trade balance
options.C: decrease Caribville's exports and increase its trade balance
options.D: decrease Caribville's exports and decrease its trade balance

questions.explanation: Selling pencils to a buyer abroad is an export from Caribville. Exports raise the value of total exports and improve (increase) the trade balance (exports minus imports).

distractors.B: Higher exports cannot worsen the trade balance.
distractors.C: Selling abroad increases exports, not decreases them.
distractors.D: A sale abroad is an export, not a fall in exports.

questions.stem: Patrick sends money to his sister who lives abroad. This transaction is recorded in the
questions.explanation: Personal remittances (money sent abroad to family) are unrequited current transfers and are recorded in the current account of the balance of payments.

distractors.A: There is no 'fixed account' in the balance of payments.
distractors.B: The capital account records transfers of capital assets (e.g. FDI, debt forgiveness), not personal remittances.
distractors.D: 'Finance account' (financial account) records financial asset transactions; remittances are not financial-asset transactions.

questions.stem: In Country B, the balance of payments is reflecting a deficit. This could be remedied by

options.C: increasing the expatriate quota for local employers
options.D: decreasing the expatriate quota for local employers

questions.explanation: A balance-of-payments deficit means more money is leaving the country than entering it. Decreasing imports cuts the outflow while increasing exports boosts the inflow - both directly improve the balance of payments.

distractors.B: Increasing imports and decreasing exports worsens, not improves, the deficit.
distractors.C: Bringing in more foreign workers (and the payments they remit) is unlikely to remedy a BoP deficit.
distractors.D: Reducing expatriate quotas has only an indirect, ambiguous BoP effect - far weaker than direct trade adjustments.

questions.stem: Between 2013 and 2023, Country F imported 500 000 cars from the United States. This represented an increase of 100% over the previous ten-year period. As a result, the demand for US dollars increased significantly while exports to the US increased by 2%. The scenario described above is MOST likely to result in

options.A: Country F's dollar appreciating against the US dollar
options.B: Country F's dollar depreciating against the US dollar
options.C: the value of the US dollar remaining constant
options.D: the value of the US dollar falling

questions.explanation: Country F now demands far more US dollars (to pay for imports) than is being supplied by exports to the US. With high demand for US dollars relative to its supply, the US dollar rises and Country F's dollar falls (depreciates) against the US dollar.

distractors.A: Country F's currency cannot appreciate when demand for the foreign (US) currency rises sharply.
distractors.C: The US dollar would not remain constant when demand for it rises strongly.
distractors.D: The US dollar would rise (not fall) given the higher demand for it.

questions.stem: Which of the following scenarios describes a bilateral agreement?

options.A: An agreement between Jamaica and Cuba
options.B: An agreement between St Vincent and the Grenadines
options.C: An agreement between the USA, the Caribbean and Mexico
options.D: An agreement between Trinidad and Tobago and each of the other Caribbean islands

questions.explanation: A bilateral agreement is one made between exactly TWO parties (countries). An agreement between Jamaica and Cuba involves two sovereign countries, so it is bilateral.

distractors.B: St Vincent and the Grenadines is a single country (one party), not two countries.
distractors.C: An agreement involving the USA, the Caribbean and Mexico has more than two parties - it is multilateral.
distractors.D: Trinidad and Tobago with each of several other islands amounts to a multilateral/multi-party arrangement.

questions.stem: One benefit CARICOM countries enjoy from preferential trade agreements is

options.C: setting market rules for neighbouring countries
options.D: greater protection of local markets from price shocks

questions.explanation: Preferential trade agreements lower or remove tariff/non-tariff barriers between member states. This gives Caribbean countries improved access to one another's markets (and to those of preferential partners) for their exports.

distractors.A: 'Financial freedom' is not a recognised benefit of trade agreements.
distractors.C: Member states do not set market rules for non-member neighbours.
distractors.D: Preferential agreements expose, rather than insulate, local markets from external price shocks.

questions.stem: One benefit of the Caribbean Single Market and Economy (CSME) is the free movement of labour within the region. This means that a CARICOM national can

options.A: invest in businesses in any CSME member state
options.B: work in any CSME member state with a temporary work permit
options.C: work in any CSME member state without the immediate need for a work permit
options.D: relocate his business to any CSME member state where there are more productive workers

questions.explanation: Free movement of labour under the CSME allows qualifying CARICOM nationals to live and work in any participating member state without the immediate need for a work permit.

distractors.A: Investing in businesses is free movement of CAPITAL, not labour.
distractors.B: The free-movement provisions specifically remove the need for a work permit, not allow only temporary permits.
distractors.D: Relocating a BUSINESS is free movement of capital/establishment, not free movement of labour.

questions.stem: A diagram shows the supply and demand for a good in Country A. After the WTO agrees to the implementation of a tariff, two horizontal lines are shown: the world price line and a higher world-price-plus-tariff line. Quantities Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 are marked along the quantity axis (with Q1 < Q2 < Q3 < Q4). The quantity of imports before the tariff is represented by
questions.explanation: Before the tariff, the country trades at the world price. Domestic producers supply the quantity at which their supply curve meets the world price (Q1) and domestic consumers demand the quantity at which their demand curve meets the world price (Q4). Imports = total demand - domestic supply = Q4 - Q1.

distractors.B: Q3 - Q2 represents imports AFTER the tariff (a smaller gap at the higher price).
distractors.C: Q4 - Q3 represents the fall in consumption due to the tariff.
distractors.D: Q4 - Q2 does not correspond to imports either before or after the tariff.

questions.stem: An 'economy' is BEST described as a system where

options.A: suppliers produce all the goods and services needed by the consumers
options.B: all the goods and services used by the citizens of a country are provided by the government
options.C: resources are used by manufacturers to produce goods and maximize profits
options.D: resources are used to produce goods and services to meet the needs and wants of society

questions.explanation: An economy is a system through which scarce resources are allocated to produce goods and services that satisfy the needs and wants of its members. This definition captures both production and the social objective of meeting human needs.

distractors.A: Too narrow - focuses only on suppliers and ignores resource allocation.
distractors.B: Describes only a planned/command economy, not economies in general.
distractors.C: Profit maximization is a firm-level goal, not the definition of an economy.

questions.stem: The diagram shows a firm's total fixed cost (TFC), total variable cost (TVC) and total cost (TC), with cost in $000 on the vertical axis. At an output of 100 units, TFC is approximately $5 000 and TC is approximately $25 000. The firm's total variable cost at 100 units of output is
questions.explanation: By definition, TC = TFC + TVC, so TVC = TC - TFC. Reading the diagram at 100 units: TVC = 25 000 - 5 000 = 20 000.

distractors.A: Too low - would imply TC of only $15 000.
distractors.B: Would imply TC of only $20 000.
distractors.D: This is the value of TC, not TVC.

questions.stem: The economic system of MOST Caribbean countries can be described as
questions.explanation: Most Caribbean economies combine private enterprise with significant government intervention (state-owned utilities, regulation, public services). This combination is the defining feature of a mixed economy.

distractors.B: Planned economies have central state control over almost all production - not the Caribbean case.
distractors.C: Traditional economies are based on custom and subsistence, characteristic of pre-industrial societies.
distractors.D: A pure free-market system has minimal government involvement, which does not describe Caribbean economies.

questions.stem: The burden of an excise duty falls ENTIRELY on the consumer if the
questions.explanation: The side of the market that is less elastic bears more of a tax. When supply is perfectly elastic (horizontal), producers can pass the entire tax on as a higher price, so consumers pay it all.

distractors.B: If demand were perfectly elastic, consumers would refuse any price rise and producers would bear the whole tax.
distractors.C: Perfectly inelastic demand also shifts the burden to consumers, but the textbook condition tied to the supply side is the perfectly elastic supply case.
distractors.D: Perfectly inelastic supply means producers bear the entire burden, not consumers.

questions.stem:
The table shows a country's gross domestic product (GDP) for a five-year period.

| Year | GDP ($M) |
|------|----------|
| 2009 | 372 |
| 2010 | 455 |
| 2011 | 496 |
| 2012 | 531 |
| 2013 | 580 |

During the period 2009-2013, the GDP of this country
questions.explanation: GDP rose every year from $372M to $580M with no decreases. The pattern is a steady increase.

distractors.A: Values rose, they did not decline.
distractors.C: Fluctuated would require both ups and downs; here every year is higher than the last.
distractors.D: The figures are clearly changing each year, not constant.

questions.stem: In an attempt to increase the efficiency of its staff, management assigns specific tasks to each worker. This is an example of
questions.explanation: Assigning each worker a specific task is the definition of the division of labour or specialization. It allows workers to become more skilled at their narrow task and raises output.

distractors.A: 'Progress' is too vague and not a defined economic term.
distractors.B: Productivity is the result (output per worker), not the practice of assigning tasks.
distractors.D: Entrepreneurial ability relates to risk-taking and combining factors of production, not task assignment.

questions.stem: In a commercial bank, wages and salaries are NOT paid to

options.D: maintenance workers employed by the bank

questions.explanation: Shareholders own the bank's capital; they receive dividends (a share of profit), not wages or salaries. Wages and salaries are payments to labour - i.e. employees.

distractors.B: Tellers and supervisors are employees paid wages/salaries.
distractors.C: Investment managers are employees who earn salaries.
distractors.D: Maintenance workers are employees paid wages.

questions.stem: One example of a 'good' is

options.B: a loan approval by a bank
options.D: a beauty makeover at a spa

questions.explanation: A good is a tangible, physical item that can be touched and stored. A bottle of soda water is tangible. The other options are intangible activities, which are services.

distractors.A: An extra lesson is a service (teaching).
distractors.B: Loan approval is a banking service.
distractors.D: A spa makeover is a personal service.

questions.stem: A firm produces 29 pairs of high-quality leather shoes at a fixed cost of $20 000 and a variable cost of $9 000. On producing the 30th pair, the new total cost rises to $30 000. What is the marginal cost of the 30th pair?
questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the change in total cost from producing one more unit. TC at 29 pairs = $20 000 + $9 000 = $29 000. TC at 30 pairs = $30 000. MC = $30 000 - $29 000 = $1 000.

distractors.B: $9 000 is the total variable cost for 29 pairs, not the marginal cost.
distractors.C: $20 000 is the total fixed cost.
distractors.D: $29 000 is the total cost of 29 pairs, not the marginal cost.

questions.stem: One of the problems associated with barter is that

options.A: it requires the use of coins
options.B: it is a cause of market failure
options.D: it requires double coincidence of wants

questions.explanation: Barter requires that each party have exactly what the other wants and want exactly what the other has - the double coincidence of wants. This makes exchange difficult and is the main drawback that money overcomes.

distractors.A: Barter does not use coins; that is one form of money-based exchange.
distractors.B: Barter is inefficient but is not what economists mean by market failure.
distractors.C: Being able to exchange surplus output is an advantage, not a problem.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an example of a factor of production in a commercial bank?
questions.explanation: Factors of production are the resources used to produce output (land, labour, capital, enterprise). A 'special savings account for students' is a product/service offered by the bank, not an input used to produce its services.

distractors.A: The building is capital - a factor of production.
distractors.B: The vault is capital equipment used in producing banking services.
distractors.C: Shareholders provide financial capital and represent enterprise/ownership.

questions.stem: Open competition on a worldwide scale facilitates

options.A: high levels of dependence on tourism
options.B: increase in trade unions' negotiating power

questions.explanation: Worldwide open competition pushes each country to focus on what it produces relatively most efficiently. This drives the development of comparative advantage and gains from international trade.

distractors.A: Dependence on tourism is country-specific, not a general result of global competition.
distractors.B: Open competition typically weakens trade unions' bargaining power, not strengthens it.
distractors.C: Open competition limits government interference in markets, not facilitates it.

questions.stem:
The table shows the market demand and supply of potatoes at given prices.

| Price of Potatoes ($) | Market Demand ('000 kg) | Market Supply ('000 kg) |
|----------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| 4 | 700 | 100 |
| 8 | 500 | 200 |
| 12 | 350 | 350 |
| 16 | 200 | 530 |
| 20 | 100 | 700 |

Which is the equilibrium price?
questions.explanation: Equilibrium occurs where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. At $12, both demand and supply equal 350 ('000 kg), so $12 is the equilibrium price.

distractors.A: At $4 there is excess demand (700 > 100).
distractors.B: At $8 there is excess demand (500 > 200).
distractors.D: At $16 there is excess supply (530 > 200).

questions.stem: 'Globalization' may be MOST accurately defined as the

options.A: movement of technology and financial markets worldwide
options.B: movement of tangible and intangible goods among countries
options.C: international trade of goods and services including skilled workers
options.D: integration of financial services, technology and commodity markets worldwide

questions.explanation: Globalization is the increasing integration of national economies - financial services, technology, and commodity markets - into one interdependent world market. This is the broadest and most accurate definition.

distractors.A: Only mentions technology and finance, missing commodities and the idea of integration.
distractors.B: Describes international trade but not the deeper integration of markets.
distractors.C: Also describes international trade rather than full integration.

questions.stem: If Company X is engaged in the cutting of trees to obtain lumber, which is then used by Company Y to make furniture, to which sector of the economy does Company X belong?
questions.explanation: Cutting trees to harvest a raw material (lumber) is an extractive activity from nature, which falls under the primary sector. Company Y, which makes furniture, would be in the secondary sector.

distractors.A: 'Public' refers to ownership, not the sector of production.
distractors.C: Tertiary is the services sector.
distractors.D: Secondary covers manufacturing - that is Company Y, not Company X.

questions.stem: When injections of capital are greater than withdrawals, national income will
questions.explanation: Injections add to the circular flow; withdrawals remove from it. When injections > withdrawals, more is being added than taken out, so national income increases.

distractors.B: Income would decrease only if withdrawals exceeded injections.
distractors.C: Constant income requires injections = withdrawals.
distractors.D: Equilibrium occurs when injections equal withdrawals, not when one exceeds the other.

questions.stem: When a financial asset is described as liquid, it means that

options.A: it is a weak financial asset
options.B: its price or value fluctuates often
options.C: it can easily be converted into cash
options.D: it is not acceptable as a medium of exchange

questions.explanation: Liquidity refers to how easily an asset can be converted into cash without significant loss of value. Cash itself is the most liquid asset.

distractors.A: Liquidity has nothing to do with how 'weak' or 'strong' an asset is.
distractors.B: Price volatility is a separate concept from liquidity.
distractors.D: Being acceptable as a medium of exchange is a feature of money, not the definition of liquidity.

questions.stem: Joe Brown first sells his produce at the market and then saves his money until he has enough to buy a tractor. Which of the following functions is money performing in the second case?
questions.explanation: Saving money over time so it can be used later means money is holding its value across time - this is the store of value function.

distractors.B: Unit of account refers to using money to express prices/values, not to saving.
distractors.C: Medium of exchange is the act of using money to buy/sell - that fits the first case (selling produce), not the second.
distractors.D: Standard of deferred payment relates to settling future debts, not saving for a purchase.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a function of the financial sector?

options.A: Providing a safe place for saving
options.C: Facilitating payments for goods and services
options.D: Making credit available to households and firms

questions.explanation: The financial sector provides liquidity (channels funds, allows conversion of assets into cash); it certainly does not prevent liquidity. Options A, C and D are all genuine functions.

distractors.A: Safe-keeping of savings IS a core function.
distractors.C: Facilitating payments (cheques, transfers, electronic payments) IS a function.
distractors.D: Granting credit IS a key function of banks and other financial institutions.

questions.stem: If Country X has a comparative advantage in the production of computers over Country Y, then

options.A: Country X will export all its computers
options.B: neither Country X nor Country Y should specialize in the production of computers
options.C: the opportunity cost of producing computers is higher in Country X than in Country Y
options.D: the opportunity cost of producing computers is lower in Country X than in Country Y

questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is defined in terms of opportunity cost. Country X has a comparative advantage in computers precisely because it can produce them at a lower opportunity cost than Country Y.

distractors.A: Having a comparative advantage does not mean exporting all production; the country still keeps some for itself.
distractors.B: Comparative advantage is the reason a country should specialize, not avoid it.
distractors.C: This is the reverse of the correct definition.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a financial institution in an economy?
questions.explanation: A trade union is an organisation of workers that bargains over pay and conditions - it does not handle financial intermediation, savings or loans. The other three are financial institutions.

distractors.B: Credit unions accept deposits and grant loans - they are financial institutions.
distractors.C: Commercial banks are the most common financial institutions.
distractors.D: Development banks provide long-term finance for development projects.

questions.stem: Fred earns his money by occasionally doing jobs such as painting, repairing and cleaning, while Wilma earns her money from occasional babysitting. In which sector would Fred and Wilma's activities be located?
questions.explanation: Casual, irregular work that is typically untaxed, unregistered and unrecorded is part of the informal sector. Fred and Wilma are doing odd jobs without formal employment contracts.

distractors.A: Formal-sector work has employment contracts, regular wages and tax records.
distractors.B: The foreign sector refers to international transactions.
distractors.D: The financial sector includes banks and other financial institutions.

questions.stem:
The table shows the flow of money in an economy.

| Flow | X | Y |
|------|---|---|
| I | Borrowers | Savers |
| II | Savers | Borrowers |
| III | Lenders | Borrowers |
| IV | Borrowers | Lenders |

The flow labelled III represents
questions.explanation: Flow III moves from lenders to borrowers. Money lent out for use by borrowers is known as loanable funds.

distractors.B: National income flows between firms and households, not between lenders and borrowers as such.
distractors.C: Goods and services flow from firms to households, not from lenders to borrowers.
distractors.D: Factors of production flow from households to firms.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of international trade?
questions.explanation: International trade brings benefits such as larger markets, more product choices and competitive pressure on local firms. However, greater dependency on other countries is widely seen as a disadvantage, not a benefit.

distractors.A: Larger market size IS a benefit - more customers for exporters.
distractors.B: Variety of products available to consumers IS a benefit.
distractors.C: Competition with foreign firms tends to raise local efficiency - IS a benefit.

questions.stem:
Which of the following situations would lead to an improvement in a country's terms of trade?

I. Export prices rise and import prices fall
II. Export prices rise faster than import prices
III. Import prices rise faster than export prices
questions.explanation: Terms of trade = (Index of export prices / Index of import prices) x 100. The ratio improves when export prices rise relative to import prices. This happens in I and II. In III, import prices rising faster than export prices makes the ratio fall - that is a worsening of the terms of trade.

distractors.A: Excludes II, which also improves the terms of trade.
distractors.C: III actually worsens, not improves, the terms of trade.
distractors.D: Includes III (which worsens TOT) and omits I.

questions.stem: The International Monetary Fund (IMF) functions as a

options.C: large, multinational corporation maximizing its efficiency through globalization
options.D: financial institution that provides strategic guidance to countries to ensure financial stability

questions.explanation: The IMF is an international financial institution that provides loans, surveillance and policy advice to member countries to help maintain financial and exchange-rate stability.

distractors.A: Human rights work is the role of bodies such as the UN, not the IMF.
distractors.B: Regulating international trade is the role of the World Trade Organization (WTO).
distractors.C: The IMF is not a profit-seeking corporation.

questions.stem: If a country can produce 20 units of product X or 10 units of product Y, the opportunity cost of 1 unit of product Y is
questions.explanation: 20X = 10Y, so 1Y = 20X / 10 = 2 units of X. To gain one unit of Y, the country must give up 2 units of X.

distractors.A: 0.1 X would correspond to the opportunity cost of 1 X in terms of Y, not vice versa.
distractors.B: 1 X would only be correct if 20X = 20Y, which is not the case.
distractors.D: 2.5 X is not consistent with the production figures given.

questions.stem: Which of the following phrases BEST explains the term 'tariff'?

options.A: The amount of subsidies received by local exporters
options.B: The level of tax that is levied on imports entering a country
options.C: The amount of goods that can be imported into a country
options.D: The common tax levied by countries on member countries within a region

questions.explanation: A tariff is a tax placed on imported goods. It raises the price of imports, making domestic substitutes relatively cheaper.

distractors.A: Describes export subsidies, not tariffs.
distractors.C: Describes a quota - a quantity limit, not a tax.
distractors.D: Describes a common external tariff, a specific feature of customs unions, not the general definition of a tariff.

questions.stem: Which of the following will contribute to a surplus on the balance of trade of a country?

options.A: The value of exports is greater than the value of imports
options.B: The value of imports is greater than the value of exports
options.C: The volume of exports is greater than the volume of imports
options.D: The volume of imports is greater than the volume of exports

questions.explanation: The balance of trade is measured by the value of exports minus the value of imports. A surplus occurs when the value of exports exceeds the value of imports.

distractors.B: This describes a trade deficit, not a surplus.
distractors.C: Volume alone is not used; prices matter. A higher export volume at low prices may still leave a deficit.
distractors.D: Again, volume is not the measure of the balance of trade.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a determinant of demand for laptop computers?
questions.explanation: The price of laptops itself causes a movement along the demand curve, not a shift of it. The non-price factors that shift demand (determinants) include income, tastes/preferences, prices of related goods, and expectations. The own price is therefore not classed as a determinant of demand.

distractors.A: Income is a key determinant of demand for normal goods like laptops.
distractors.C: A tax on laptops effectively changes the price consumers pay - it acts through the supply side rather than as a non-price determinant of demand.
distractors.D: Preference for desktops (a substitute) is a related-good preference and is a determinant of demand for laptops.

questions.stem: The central bank calls for an increase in the reserve requirements of commercial banks. How will this affect the operations of commercial banks?

options.A: Their liquid assets will be reduced.
options.B: Their ability to create credit will increase.
options.C: Their loans to money markets will increase.
options.D: They will attract less deposits from the public.

questions.explanation: A higher reserve requirement forces banks to keep more cash/deposits with the central bank and lend out less. This reduces the liquid assets the banks have available to use and reduces their ability to create credit.

distractors.B: Higher reserves reduce - not increase - the ability to create credit.
distractors.C: With less to lend, loans to money markets would fall, not rise.
distractors.D: Reserve requirements do not directly determine the volume of deposits attracted from the public.

questions.stem: Country A is experiencing a balance of payments deficit. Which of the following measures could be taken to reduce the deficit in the short term?

options.A: Reduce the volume of exports to other countries.
options.B: Engage in trade with other low-cost producers of goods.
options.C: Increase the importation of goods from other countries.
options.D: Impose restrictions on goods imported from other countries.

questions.explanation: To narrow a deficit quickly, the country must reduce imports or boost exports. Imposing restrictions (tariffs, quotas) on imports cuts spending on foreign goods and helps reduce the deficit in the short term.

distractors.A: Reducing exports would worsen the deficit, not reduce it.
distractors.B: Buying from cheaper producers may help marginally but is not the standard short-term policy tool.
distractors.C: More imports would deepen the deficit.

questions.stem: Which of the following is MOST likely to lead to greater international investment flows to the Caribbean region?
questions.explanation: Globalization opens up cross-border movement of capital, technology and businesses. The Caribbean stands to receive more foreign direct investment as global integration deepens.

distractors.B: Specialization affects what is produced, not directly the flow of foreign investment.
distractors.C: Trade unionism can actually deter foreign investment if it raises labour costs.
distractors.D: Currency fluctuation introduces uncertainty and can discourage, not encourage, investment.

questions.stem: The acronym 'FDI' stands for
questions.explanation: FDI stands for Foreign Direct Investment - investment by a firm or individual from one country into business interests located in another country, typically through ownership of productive assets.

distractors.A: 'Foreign Direct Income' is not a standard term.
distractors.C: Fixed Domestic Investment is not what FDI stands for.
distractors.D: Floating Domestic Investment is not a recognised term.

questions.stem: One example of an 'entrepreneur' is
questions.explanation: An entrepreneur owns the business, combines the factors of production and bears the risk. A shop owner fits this description. The other options are employees who work for entrepreneurs.

distractors.B: A store worker is an employee earning a wage.
distractors.C: A bank manager is a senior employee, not the owner.
distractors.D: An airline employee works for the firm; they do not bear the business risk.
